heroOverview: Cryoglobulinemia is a condition in which immunoglobulins (cryoglobulins) that reversibly precipitate at temperatures below 37°C circulate in the blood, deposit in small blood vessels, and trigger complement activation and vasculitis. The clinical hallmarks are purpura on the lower legs, arthralgia, peripheral neuropathy, and glomerulonephritis — a tetrad first described by Meltzer and Franklin in 1966. Hepatitis C virus infection drives more than 80% of all mixed cryoglobulinemia cases worldwide, and achieving sustained viral clearance with direct-acting antivirals resolves or markedly improves symptoms in the majority. Less commonly, cryoglobulinemia arises in haematological malignancies (type I) or systemic autoimmune diseases. Globally, cryoglobulinemia affects an estimated 1-3% of people with chronic hepatitis C, representing hundreds of thousands of cases, though it is underdiagnosed because symptoms are often attributed to the underlying disease alone.

keyStats.prevalence: Detectable cryoglobulins in serum in 40-60% of hepatitis C patients; symptomatic mixed cryoglobulinemia vasculitis in approximately 5-10% of HCV-infected individuals
keyStats.demographics: Adults predominantly; hepatitis C-associated type peaks in middle-aged adults; haematological causes more common in older adults; women slightly more often than men in type II/III
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at diagnosis approximately 45-55 years in HCV-associated cases
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 500,000-1,000,000 people worldwide have symptomatic cryoglobulinemia at any given time

definition: Cryoglobulinemia (ICD-10: D89.1) is defined by the presence in serum of one or more immunoglobulins that form a precipitate or gel below 37°C and redissolve on warming. The Brouet classification divides cryoglobulins into three types: type I consists of a single monoclonal immunoglobulin (IgG or IgM), typically associated with plasma cell dyscrasias (multiple myeloma, MGUS) or Waldenström macroglobulinaemia; type II is mixed cryoglobulinemia with a monoclonal IgM with rheumatoid factor activity directed against polyclonal IgG, most often driven by hepatitis C virus; type III consists of polyclonal immunoglobulins, seen in systemic autoimmune diseases. Types II and III together are termed mixed cryoglobulinemia. The pathophysiology centres on cold-induced precipitation of cryoglobulin complexes in small vessels, complement consumption (causing low C4 with normal C3 — a diagnostic pointer), and immune complex-mediated inflammation producing leucocytoclastic vasculitis in skin, nerves, and kidneys. The condition spans haematology, rheumatology, and nephrology depending on which organ is predominantly affected. Without treatment, renal cryoglobulinemic glomerulonephritis can progress to end-stage renal disease, and lymphoma develops in approximately 8-10% of type II patients with chronic HCV infection over 10 years.

typesClassification.description: A single monoclonal immunoglobulin associated with multiple myeloma, MGUS, or Waldenström macroglobulinaemia; presents mainly with hyperviscosity syndrome, Raynaud phenomenon, and skin necrosis rather than the vasculitic features of types II and III.

Monoclonal IgM with rheumatoid factor activity plus polyclonal IgG; the most common clinical type, almost invariably associated with hepatitis C virus; produces immune complex vasculitis affecting skin, nerves, and kidneys.

All polyclonal immunoglobulins; associated with autoimmune diseases (SLE, Sjögren syndrome, rheumatoid arthritis) and occasionally other chronic infections; generally a milder clinical course than type II.

primarySymptoms: Purpura — palpable, non-blanching purple or red spots — on the lower legs and feet, the most common presentation in mixed cryoglobulinemia, caused by leucocytoclastic vasculitis of dermal small vessels; occurs in 70-90% of symptomatic patients.
Arthralgia affecting multiple joints symmetrically without frank synovitis, present in 70-80% of patients; joints are sore and stiff but typically not deformed.

Peripheral sensorimotor neuropathy causing numbness, tingling, burning sensations, and weakness in a stocking-glove distribution; affects 40-60% of patients and can cause significant functional impairment.
Fatigue and weakness, reported by over 80% of patients, resulting from both the vasculitic process and the underlying hepatitis C or autoimmune disease.
Renal involvement manifesting as microscopic haematuria, proteinuria, or frank nephrotic or nephritic syndrome from membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis; affects 20-30% of symptomatic patients.
Raynaud phenomenon — episodic blue or white colour change of the fingers and toes in response to cold — particularly prominent in type I and some type II cases.
Skin ulceration on the lower legs or feet from severe leucocytoclastic vasculitis when purpura is extensive and confluent.
Sicca symptoms — dry eyes and dry mouth — when cryoglobulinemia occurs in the setting of Sjögren syndrome.
Abdominal pain from mesenteric vasculitis in severe cases, a rare but dangerous manifestation requiring urgent assessment.
earlyWarningSigns: New purpuric lesions on the lower legs in a patient with known hepatitis C, which warrant immediate cryoglobulin serology testing.
Unexplained low serum C4 with normal C3 on complement testing — a specific pointer to immune complex consumption by cryoglobulin.
Mild persistent proteinuria on routine urinalysis in a hepatitis C or autoimmune disease patient.
Gradual onset of numbness or paraesthesiae in the feet not explained by diabetes or other obvious cause.
emergencySigns: Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis with rising creatinine, haematuria, and heavy proteinuria — risk of irreversible kidney failure without immediate immunosuppressive treatment.
Extensive confluent purpura with skin ulceration suggesting severe systemic vasculitis.
Acute onset of central nervous system symptoms (stroke, confusion, seizures) from cerebral vasculitis, a rare but life-threatening manifestation.
Severe abdominal pain with absent bowel sounds suggesting mesenteric infarction from vasculitis.

causes.description: HCV drives the production of mixed cryoglobulins — primarily type II — by chronically stimulating B cells to produce rheumatoid factor-active IgM that complexes with viral antigens and polyclonal IgG. Detectable cryoglobulins are found in 40-60% of HCV-infected patients; symptomatic vasculitis develops in approximately 5-10%, with the risk increasing with duration of infection and higher viral loads.

Multiple myeloma, Waldenström macroglobulinaemia, MGUS, and B-cell lymphomas produce monoclonal immunoglobulins that intrinsically precipitate in the cold (type I cryoglobulinemia). The monoclonal protein is both the cryoglobulin and the tumour marker, and treatment of the malignancy typically resolves cryoglobulinemic symptoms.

SLE produces a chronic polyclonal immunoglobulin excess and immune complex formation that can generate type III cryoglobulins; cryoglobulinemia occurs in approximately 15-20% of SLE patients and may exacerbate lupus nephritis.

The chronic B-cell hyperactivation of Sjögren syndrome is a well-recognised cause of type II cryoglobulinemia and the associated vasculitis; importantly, Sjögren-related cryoglobulinemia also carries an elevated risk of non-Hodgkin lymphoma transformation.

Hepatitis B virus, HIV, subacute bacterial endocarditis, and leishmaniasis can trigger cryoglobulin production by sustaining chronic B-cell antigenic stimulation similar to HCV, though far less commonly; HCV coinfection often drives cryoglobulinemia in HIV patients.

In approximately 5-10% of mixed cryoglobulinemia cases, no underlying cause is identified after thorough investigation; some of these represent undetected or resolved HCV infection, and others may be occult lymphoproliferative diseases that become apparent over follow-up.

riskFactors.description: The dominant risk factor for type II mixed cryoglobulinemia; risk rises with increasing duration of HCV infection, higher HCV RNA levels, and HCV genotype 2 in some series. Curative antiviral treatment eliminates the stimulus for cryoglobulin production.

riskFactors.factor: Plasma cell dyscrasia or B-cell malignancy
riskFactors.description: Multiple myeloma, MGUS, Waldenström macroglobulinaemia, and B-cell lymphoma directly produce monoclonal cryoglobulins (type I); cryoglobulinemia is a haematological complication requiring treatment of the underlying malignancy.
Chronic B-cell activation in these diseases generates polyclonal immunoglobulins that form type III cryoglobulins; the risk of vasculitic complications correlates with disease activity.
Mixed cryoglobulinemia occurs slightly more commonly in women, consistent with the female predominance of underlying HCV-associated liver disease and autoimmune conditions.

riskFactors.factor: Cold climate or occupational cold exposure
riskFactors.description: Cold exposure precipitates cryoglobulins in vivo, potentially worsening Raynaud phenomenon, skin necrosis, and acute cryoglobulinemia flares, particularly in type I disease.
HIV further activates B cells and dysregulates humoral immunity, amplifying HCV-driven cryoglobulin production and increasing the risk of symptomatic vasculitis in coinfected individuals.

affectedDemographics: Middle-aged adults with chronic hepatitis C infection, particularly those infected for more than 10 years
Older adults with haematological malignancies producing monoclonal immunoglobulins
Women with systemic lupus erythematosus or Sjögren syndrome
Patients with idiopathic mixed cryoglobulinemia when no primary cause is found
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis requires demonstrating cryoglobulins in the serum and correlating them with the clinical syndrome. Blood must be collected and maintained at 37°C before allowing to clot at 37°C and then centrifuging at room temperature; chilling during transport causes in vitro precipitation that loses cryoglobulins in the clot and produces a false-negative result — a common laboratory pitfall. The cryocrit (percentage of serum volume occupied by the precipitate) and immunofixation electrophoresis of the redissolved cryoprecipitate identify the type: type I shows a single monoclonal band; type II shows monoclonal IgM plus polyclonal IgG; type III shows polyclonal components only. Complement levels are a valuable clue: low C4 with normal or mildly reduced C3 is the classical pattern reflecting classical pathway consumption by immune complexes. Rheumatoid factor is positive in virtually all type II cases. A comprehensive diagnostic work-up includes: HCV RNA and genotype by PCR; HBsAg and anti-HBc; HIV test; ANA, anti-dsDNA, and anti-Ro/La for autoimmune disease; serum protein electrophoresis and immunofixation for plasma cell dyscrasia; renal function, urine protein-creatinine ratio, and urinalysis for renal involvement. Nerve conduction studies quantify neuropathy. Skin or renal biopsy showing leucocytoclastic vasculitis or membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis with IgM and C3 deposits on immunofluorescence confirms the tissue diagnosis. Risk stratification using the CryoVas score or French Vasculitis Study Group criteria guides treatment intensity.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates cryoglobulin presence and typing (I, II, or III); must be collected and processed at 37°C to avoid false negatives from cold precipitation during phlebotomy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood drawn into pre-warmed tubes and maintained at 37°C throughout processing; cryoprecipitate is quantified after refrigeration for 7 days and then typed; results in 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Low C4 with normal C3 is the classical fingerprint of immune complex-mediated complement consumption in mixed cryoglobulinemia; a normal C4 argues against active cryoglobulinemia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine blood draw; results within 24 hours; C4 < 0.1 g/L strongly supports the diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.test: Hepatitis C serology and HCV RNA quantification
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the most common underlying cause; HCV RNA confirms active infection and quantifies viral load, guiding antiviral treatment decisions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; HCV antibody result in hours; HCV RNA PCR result in 1-3 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reflects the presence of IgM with rheumatoid factor activity in type II cryoglobulinemia; positive in virtually 100% of type II cases; high titres are typical.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; result within 24 hours; must be interpreted alongside cryoglobulin typing.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies monoclonal immunoglobulin bands pointing to myeloma, MGUS, or Waldenström macroglobulinaemia as the cause in type I cryoglobulinemia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; gel electrophoresis and immunofixation of serum returned in 3-5 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Urinalysis, urine protein-creatinine ratio, and renal function
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for cryoglobulinemic nephritis; haematuria, casts, and proteinuria indicate renal involvement warranting biopsy and urgent treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Simple urine sample plus blood draw; results within 24 hours; repeated at each clinic visit in patients with known renal involvement.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the pattern and severity of peripheral neuropathy; typically shows a predominantly axonal sensorimotor neuropathy with predominant sensory involvement.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-invasive electrodes applied to the skin; mild discomfort from small electrical stimuli; takes 30-60 minutes; performed by a neurologist or clinical physiologist.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is guided by cryoglobulinemia type, the underlying cause, and organ involvement severity. For the majority of patients — those with HCV-associated type II mixed cryoglobulinemia — achieving sustained virological response (SVR) with direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) is the primary and most effective treatment. Current DAA regimens (sofosbuvir-based combinations, glecaprevir-pibrentasvir) cure HCV in over 95% of patients regardless of genotype, and SVR is followed by cryoglobulin disappearance or substantial reduction in 70-90% of cases, with improvement in purpura, arthralgia, and neuropathy over months. For severe or life-threatening vasculitis — rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, extensive cutaneous ulceration, or severe neuropathy — immunosuppressive therapy is added, typically with rituximab (an anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody) that depletes the B-cell clones producing cryoglobulins. Rituximab achieves clinical responses in 60-80% of refractory or severe cases. Corticosteroids provide rapid anti-inflammatory bridging while rituximab or antivirals take effect. Plasma exchange (plasmapheresis) rapidly reduces circulating cryoglobulin levels and is reserved for organ-threatening emergencies such as rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis or hyperviscosity. For type I cryoglobulinemia, treatment targets the underlying haematological malignancy — myeloma or Waldenström therapy typically resolves the cryoglobulinemia. Hydroxychloroquine and NSAIDs help mild musculoskeletal symptoms. Body warmth and avoidance of cold exposure are important non-pharmacological measures to prevent in vivo precipitation.

medicalTreatments.name: Direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) for HCV
medicalTreatments.description: Pan-genotypic combinations such as sofosbuvir/velpatasvir or glecaprevir/pibrentasvir taken orally for 8-12 weeks; achieve SVR in over 95% of patients regardless of HCV genotype; SVR is the primary treatment goal in all HCV-associated cryoglobulinemia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: SVR rates above 95% with modern DAAs; cryoglobulin levels fall in 70-90% of patients achieving SVR; clinical improvement in purpura, arthralgia, and proteinuria in the majority within 6-24 months.

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab (anti-CD20)
medicalTreatments.description: Intravenous monoclonal antibody depleting CD20-positive B cells, including the rheumatoid-factor-producing B-cell clones responsible for cryoglobulin production; used for severe or organ-threatening vasculitis, usually combined with antiviral therapy in HCV-positive patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical response (purpura, neuropathy, renal improvement) in approximately 60-80% of treated patients; cryoglobulin levels fall in most responders; median duration of response approximately 12-18 months.

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroids (prednisolone or methylprednisolone)
medicalTreatments.description: High-dose steroids used as bridging therapy for severe acute flares while awaiting rituximab response or antiviral effect; pulse intravenous methylprednisolone (1 g/day for 3 days) is used in organ-threatening presentations.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapidly suppresses acute inflammation in most patients; not curative; prolonged use avoided because of immunosuppression worsening the underlying HCV infection and standard steroid side effects.

medicalTreatments.name: Plasma exchange (plasmapheresis)
medicalTreatments.description: Mechanical removal of cryoglobulin-containing plasma, replaced with fresh frozen plasma or albumin; used in emergencies such as rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis or hyperviscosity syndrome to rapidly reduce cryoglobulin burden while longer-term treatments take effect.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces serum cryoglobulin levels by 50-70% per session; effect is temporary (days to weeks) unless combined with definitive therapy; typically 3-5 sessions performed on alternate days.

medicalTreatments.name: Hydroxychloroquine
medicalTreatments.description: An antimalarial with immune-modulating properties; used for mild-to-moderate mixed cryoglobulinemia with primarily musculoskeletal symptoms; helps reduce joint pain and may modestly reduce cryoglobulin levels.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces arthralgia and constitutional symptoms in approximately 40-60% of mild-moderate cases; data from controlled trials are limited.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cold avoidance and body warmth
alternativeTreatments.description: Maintaining ambient warmth, wearing gloves and layered clothing in cold environments, and avoiding cold water exposure reduces in vivo cryoglobulin precipitation, particularly important for type I disease with prominent Raynaud phenomenon and cold-provoked purpura.

alternativeTreatments.name: Colchicine
alternativeTreatments.description: Used in some centres for mild cutaneous vasculitis manifestations; its anti-inflammatory mechanism reduces neutrophil-mediated tissue damage in leucocytoclastic vasculitis.

whySeeSpecialist: Cryoglobulinemia requires a multidisciplinary approach. A haematologist evaluates for underlying lymphoproliferative disease. A hepatologist or infectious disease specialist manages HCV treatment. A rheumatologist coordinates immunosuppressive therapy and monitors autoimmune disease. A nephrologist is essential when renal involvement is present. This multi-organ condition rarely responds optimally to single-specialty management.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a haematologist with expertise in lymphoproliferative diseases or a rheumatologist specialising in systemic vasculitis, depending on which organ system is most affected. The treating team should include access to a hepatologist familiar with DAA regimens for HCV, a nephrologist for renal disease management, and a neurologist for neuropathy assessment. Ask whether the centre has experience with rituximab protocols for cryoglobulinemia and with plasma exchange capability for severe cases.
preventionStrategies: Treat hepatitis C with direct-acting antivirals before cryoglobulinemia complications develop; HCV cure eliminates the primary stimulus for type II cryoglobulin production.
Screen HCV-infected patients annually for cryoglobulin and complement levels to detect developing disease early.
Keep body warm and avoid cold environments to reduce precipitation of cryoglobulins in skin vessels.
For haematological malignancies, ensure primary disease treatment is optimised to reduce monoclonal protein production.
Vaccinate against hepatitis B and use appropriate precautions to prevent HCV acquisition.
lifestyleModifications: Keep the home and workplace warm; use heated gloves and socks in cold weather.
Avoid cold water, ice packs, and cold food consumption that may trigger oral or pharyngeal cryoglobulin precipitation in severe disease.
Protect lower-leg skin from trauma; even minor wounds can develop into ulcers with impaired microcirculation.
Rest affected limbs when purpura is active and raise legs to reduce dependent oedema.
Avoid smoking, which worsens peripheral circulation and vasospasm.
Limit alcohol to protect the liver in HCV-associated disease.

recommended: Balanced diet supporting renal health (adequate protein but not excessive) when nephritis is present
Anti-inflammatory foods — Mediterranean-pattern diet — may provide modest symptomatic benefit
Adequate hydration to support renal function
avoid: Alcohol, which worsens liver function in HCV-associated disease and may interact with antiviral medications
Excessive sodium and protein if significant proteinuria or renal impairment is present
Very cold food and drinks, which may trigger oral cryoglobulin precipitation in severe type I disease

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate exercise is generally safe and helps maintain mobility and mood. Avoid exercising in cold weather outdoors without adequate protective clothing. During acute flares with active purpura, vasculitis, or severe neuropathy, rest affected limbs and limit high-impact activity. After achieving HCV cure or disease remission, progressive aerobic exercise improves fatigue and cardiovascular health.
dailyManagement: Take antiviral medications consistently at the same time daily; missed doses risk viral rebound and loss of response.
Check the lower legs daily for new purpuric lesions or ulceration and report promptly.
Monitor blood pressure and urinalysis regularly if renal involvement has been detected.
Keep warm in cold weather and report any sudden deterioration in Raynaud symptoms.
Attend follow-up liver and kidney function tests and cryoglobulin monitoring as scheduled.
prognosis: The prognosis of cryoglobulinemia has improved dramatically since the introduction of direct-acting antivirals for hepatitis C. Among HCV-associated mixed cryoglobulinemia patients who achieve SVR, 70-90% experience sustained clinical improvement and cryoglobulin disappearance, with long-term outcomes comparable to HCV patients without cryoglobulinemia. The main concerns in this group are lymphoma development — occurring in approximately 8-10% of type II patients over 10 years — and persistent neuropathy, which may not fully reverse even after viral cure. Renal disease carries the worst organ-specific prognosis: approximately 15-30% of patients with cryoglobulinemic nephritis progress to end-stage renal disease over 10 years despite treatment. Type I cryoglobulinemia prognosis depends entirely on the underlying haematological malignancy. Overall, 10-year survival in mixed cryoglobulinemia ranges from 55-75% in contemporary series, with renal failure and lymphoma transformation being the major causes of death.
recoveryTimeline: Following DAA treatment, HCV RNA becomes undetectable within 4-12 weeks; SVR is confirmed at 12 weeks post-treatment. Cryoglobulin levels begin to fall over months and may become undetectable by 12-24 months. Purpura typically improves within weeks to months of viral clearance. Neuropathy improvement is slower — partial improvement over 1-3 years, and some patients have permanent residual deficits. Renal function stabilises or improves in most patients achieving SVR, but recovery of lost renal function is incomplete. After rituximab, responses are seen within 1-3 months; re-treatment is feasible after 6 months if relapse occurs.
complications: Non-Hodgkin lymphoma transformation, occurring in approximately 8-10% of type II HCV-associated cryoglobulinemia patients over 10 years.
Progressive cryoglobulinemic glomerulonephritis advancing to end-stage renal disease in 15-30% of patients with renal involvement.
Permanent peripheral neuropathy from axonal damage that does not fully reverse even after successful antiviral treatment.
Severe cutaneous vasculitis causing extensive leg ulceration and requiring wound care or skin grafting.
Hyperviscosity syndrome in type I cryoglobulinemia, causing neurological symptoms, visual changes, and bleeding risk.

supportResources.name: AASLD — Hepatitis C Guidance
supportResources.description: American Association for the Study of Liver Diseases guidance on hepatitis C treatment, the primary cause of mixed cryoglobulinemia, including management of extrahepatic manifestations.

supportResources.name: NHS — Vasculitis
supportResources.description: UK patient information on vasculitis, which is the mechanism of organ damage in cryoglobulinemia.

supportResources.name: Vasculitis Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient support organisation for vasculitic conditions including cryoglobulinemia, with educational resources and patient community.

sources.title: Ferri C et al. Mixed cryoglobulinemia: demographic, clinical, and serological features and survival in 231 patients. Seminars in Arthritis and Rheumatism 2004
Cacoub P et al. Mixed cryoglobulinemia vasculitis: clinical and biological description with the French Vasculitis Study Group. NEJM correspondence 2011 — refer to Lancet 2016 cryoglobulinemia review
Gragnani L et al. Direct-acting antivirals for HCV-associated mixed cryoglobulinemia vasculitis. Arthritis & Rheumatology 2016
De Vita S et al. Efficacy of anti-CD20 in hepatitis C virus-associated cryoglobulinaemia. Annals of the Rheumatic Diseases 2012
EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines: Management of Hepatitis C Virus Infection. Journal of Hepatology 2020
Monti G et al. Prevalence and characteristics of non-Hodgkin lymphomas in patients with mixed cryoglobulinemia. Arthritis & Rheumatism 2005

confusedWithConditions.name: ANCA-associated vasculitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: ANCA vasculitis is a medium/small vessel autoimmune vasculitis driven by anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies; cryoglobulinemia vasculitis is driven by immune complex deposition; complement levels are low in cryoglobulinemia but typically normal in ANCA vasculitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lupus nephritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lupus nephritis and cryoglobulinemic nephritis both cause membranoproliferative patterns on biopsy and low complement, but lupus nephritis features ANA and anti-dsDNA antibodies rather than cryoglobulins and rheumatoid factor.

confusedWithConditions.name: Haemolytic uraemic syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: HUS causes microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and renal failure through thrombotic microangiopathy; cryoglobulinemia causes immune complex vasculitis without microangiopathic red cell destruction or the thrombocytopenia profile of HUS.

relatedConditions.name: Hepatitis C
relatedConditions.relevance: Hepatitis C virus is the most common cause of type II mixed cryoglobulinemia worldwide, driving over 80% of cases.

relatedConditions.name: Waldenström macroglobulinaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: A major cause of type I cryoglobulinemia; the monoclonal IgM it produces is often a cryoglobulin.

relatedConditions.name: Multiple myeloma
relatedConditions.relevance: Produces monoclonal immunoglobulins that can precipitate in the cold, causing type I cryoglobulinemia.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic B-cell activation and high rheumatoid factor titres in RA can produce type III cryoglobulins as an extrahepatic manifestation.

faqs.question: What is cryoglobulinemia?
faqs.answer: Cryoglobulinemia means proteins called cryoglobulins are circulating in the blood. They precipitate — thicken and clump — at temperatures below body temperature and deposit in small blood vessels, causing inflammation and vasculitis in skin, nerves, and kidneys.

faqs.question: What causes cryoglobulinemia?
faqs.answer: Hepatitis C virus drives over 80% of mixed cryoglobulinemia cases worldwide by chronically stimulating B cells to produce abnormal immunoglobulins. Less common causes include blood cancers (multiple myeloma, Waldenström macroglobulinaemia) and autoimmune diseases (lupus, Sjögren syndrome).

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of cryoglobulinemia?
faqs.answer: The classic triad is purpura on the lower legs, joint pain, and fatigue. Many patients also develop numbness or tingling from peripheral neuropathy and protein in the urine from kidney inflammation. Raynaud phenomenon — cold-triggered colour changes in fingers — is common in some types.

faqs.question: How is cryoglobulinemia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires a blood test for cryoglobulins, collected and processed at body temperature to prevent false negatives. Low serum C4 complement and high rheumatoid factor strongly support the diagnosis. Hepatitis C serology, protein electrophoresis, and kidney and nerve tests assess cause and organ damage.

faqs.question: Can cryoglobulinemia be cured?
faqs.answer: When caused by hepatitis C, curing the virus with direct-acting antivirals leads to resolution of cryoglobulins and clinical improvement in 70-90% of patients. When caused by a blood cancer, treating the cancer usually resolves it. Autoimmune-related cases are managed rather than cured.

faqs.question: What is mixed cryoglobulinemia?
faqs.answer: Mixed cryoglobulinemia (types II and III) involves more than one type of immunoglobulin in the cryoprecipitate, in contrast to type I which is a single monoclonal protein. Type II, the most common, is almost always associated with hepatitis C and causes the purpura, neuropathy, and kidney disease syndrome.

faqs.question: Why does my C4 come back low on blood tests?
faqs.answer: A low C4 complement level with normal C3 is the signature pattern of mixed cryoglobulinemia. Cryoglobulin-IgG immune complexes activate the classical complement pathway, consuming C4 preferentially. It serves as a useful disease activity marker — levels often normalise after hepatitis C cure or immunosuppressive treatment.

faqs.question: Does hepatitis C treatment cure cryoglobulinemia?
faqs.answer: Yes, in most patients. Modern direct-acting antivirals cure hepatitis C in over 95% of people. After viral clearance, cryoglobulin levels fall in 70-90% of patients and purpura, joint pain, and kidney inflammation typically improve over months. It is the most effective treatment available for HCV-associated cryoglobulinemia.

faqs.question: What is rituximab and when is it used?
faqs.answer: Rituximab is an intravenous medicine that destroys B cells — the cells that produce the abnormal cryoglobulin antibodies. It is used in cryoglobulinemia when the vasculitis is severe, particularly with rapidly progressive kidney disease or extensive skin ulcers, and when antiviral treatment alone is insufficient.

faqs.question: Can cryoglobulinemia cause kidney failure?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cryoglobulin deposits in the kidney glomeruli cause membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis. Without treatment, 15-30% of patients with renal involvement progress to end-stage kidney disease requiring dialysis. Early detection with urine protein testing and prompt treatment reduces this risk.

faqs.question: What is the purpura in cryoglobulinemia?
faqs.answer: Purpura are purple or red non-blanching spots on the skin, most common on the lower legs, caused by red blood cells leaking out of inflamed small blood vessels. They typically appear in crops, often triggered by prolonged standing or cold. They reflect active leucocytoclastic vasculitis.

faqs.question: Is cryoglobulinemia associated with lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Approximately 8-10% of patients with type II HCV-associated cryoglobulinemia develop non-Hodgkin lymphoma over 10 years, reflecting the chronic B-cell activation driven by the virus. Annual monitoring for lymphoma signs is recommended, and viral cure substantially reduces this risk.

faqs.question: How do I keep warm to prevent cryoglobulinemia flares?
faqs.answer: Wear gloves, layered clothing, and warm socks, particularly in cold weather. Avoid cold water exposure including cold showers. Keep the home heated. Cold-provoked precipitation of cryoglobulins worsens Raynaud phenomenon and can trigger new purpura, particularly in type I disease.

faqs.question: Can cryoglobulinemia cause nerve damage?
faqs.answer: Yes. Peripheral neuropathy from cryoglobulin vasculitis of the vasa nervorum affects 40-60% of patients. It typically causes numbness, burning, and weakness in a stocking-glove distribution. Improvement after hepatitis C treatment can be slow — 1 to 3 years — and some patients have permanent deficits.

faqs.question: What is a cryocrit?
faqs.answer: The cryocrit is the percentage of serum volume occupied by the cryoprecipitate after refrigeration. It quantifies the cryoglobulin burden; high cryocrits correlate with more severe vasculitis and are used to monitor treatment response. A falling cryocrit after antiviral therapy confirms biological activity.

faqs.question: What is the difference between type I and type II cryoglobulinemia?
faqs.answer: Type I cryoglobulinemia involves a single monoclonal immunoglobulin from a blood cancer, causing mainly hyperviscosity and cold-induced skin necrosis. Type II is mixed, with monoclonal IgM plus polyclonal IgG, almost always driven by hepatitis C, causing the classic vasculitis triad of purpura, arthralgia, and neuropathy.

faqs.question: Does cryoglobulinemia affect the liver?
faqs.answer: Directly, cryoglobulinemia does not damage the liver; the liver damage in most cases comes from the underlying hepatitis C virus. However, the portal blood flow and splanchnic vessels can be affected by severe vasculitis. Monitoring liver function is important throughout treatment.

faqs.question: How often do I need follow-up if I have cryoglobulinemia?
faqs.answer: After achieving HCV cure, monitoring every 3-6 months for at least 2 years is typical, checking cryoglobulin levels, complement, renal function, and signs of lymphoma. Patients with ongoing autoimmune-driven disease or residual organ damage are reviewed more frequently by the relevant specialist.

faqs.question: Can plasma exchange help in cryoglobulinemia?
faqs.answer: Yes, in emergencies. Plasma exchange rapidly removes cryoglobulins from the bloodstream and is used for organ-threatening presentations such as rapidly worsening kidney failure or hyperviscosity. Its effect is temporary, so it is always combined with definitive treatment such as antiviral therapy or rituximab.

faqs.question: Is cryoglobulinemia related to cold agglutinin disease?
faqs.answer: Both involve cold-reacting immunoglobulins but are distinct conditions. Cold agglutinin disease involves IgM antibodies that clump red cells at low temperatures causing haemolytic anaemia. Cryoglobulinemia involves immunoglobulins that precipitate in cold serum and damage blood vessels; red cell destruction is not the primary mechanism.

metaDescription: Cryoglobulinemia causes purpura, neuropathy, and kidney disease from cold-reactive proteins in blood. Learn about hepatitis C causes, diagnosis, and treatment.
